subsystem: wolfSSL / wolfCrypt RSA PSS signature padding. RsaPad_PSS is the EMSA-PSS encoding routine (RFC 8017 / PKCS#1 v2.2) that builds the encoded message EM = maskedDB || H || 0xbc into the caller's pkcsBlock buffer prior to the RSA private-key modular exponentiation. It hashes M' = (0x00 x 8 || mHash || salt), places that hash H, generates the random salt, builds DB, applies MGF1 as a mask, and clears the top bits so EM fits in emBits.
provenance: Identified from wolfSSL-specific identifiers: the WC_RNG / enum wc_HashType types, the XMALLOC/XMEMSET/XMEMCPY/XFREE portability macros with DYNAMIC_TYPE_RSA_BUFFER, wolfSSL error codes (MEMORY_E, BAD_FUNC_ARG, PSS_SALTLEN_E), config macros (WOLFSSL_NO_MALLOC, WOLFSSL_STATIC_MEMORY, WOLFSSL_PSS_LONG_SALT, WOLFSSL_PSS_SALT_LEN_DISCOVER, WOLFSSL_SHA512), and size macros (RSA_MAX_SIZE, RSA_PSS_PAD_SZ, WC_MAX_DIGEST_SIZE, RSA_PSS_SALT_MAX_SZ, RSA_PSS_SALT_LEN_DEFAULT/DISCOVER). The banner names it primevul_c_train_2184_fixed and classifies it under CWE-310 (cryptographic issues); this is the post-fix (hardened) revision.
trust_boundary: This is a signing-side (private-key) primitive, so the classic remote-parser trust boundary is weaker than for a wire decoder: the RSA key parameters bits and pkcsBlockLen are derived from the local signer's key, and pkcsBlock is a caller-owned output buffer. The attacker-influenceable inputs are input/inputLen (the message hash to be signed, which the function forces to equal hLen), saltLen (may come from a policy/API and in DISCOVER mode is derived from pkcsBlockLen), and hType/mgf/bits selectors. The real hazard surface is therefore the set of size invariants the CALLER must satisfy: pkcsBlock must be at least pkcsBlockLen bytes, pkcsBlockLen must equal the key's emLen, and the fixed salt/msg buffers must be large enough for the chosen hash. Any exposed API path that lets an attacker choose saltLen, bits, or pkcsBlockLen inconsistently with the actual pkcsBlock allocation turns these into memory-safety issues.

what_the_code_does.code:
static int RsaPad_PSS(const byte* input, word32 inputLen, byte* pkcsBlock,
        word32 pkcsBlockLen, WC_RNG* rng, enum wc_HashType hType, int mgf,
        int saltLen, int bits, void* heap)
what_the_code_does.explanation: The function signature of the EMSA-PSS encoder. input/inputLen carry the message hash mHash to be signed; the code later requires inputLen == digest size. pkcsBlock/pkcsBlockLen are the output buffer and its length (the encoded-message length emLen, essentially the RSA modulus size in bytes). rng is the wolfCrypt RNG used to generate the PSS salt. hType selects the hash algorithm and mgf selects the mask-generation function. saltLen is the requested salt length (with sentinel values RSA_PSS_SALT_LEN_DEFAULT and RSA_PSS_SALT_LEN_DISCOVER). bits is the RSA modulus bit length, used to compute how many high bits of the leading octet must be cleared. heap is the wolfSSL heap hint for XMALLOC/XFREE. Being static, it is an internal helper invoked by the public RSA-PSS sign path.

Local state. ret accumulates the return status. hLen is the digest size, i a loop counter, o an offset into the salt region, maskLen the length of the DB/masked region, hiBits the number of leftmost bits to clear. m and s are working cursors into the scratch buffer used to assemble M'. The msg buffer is chosen by build configuration: when malloc is disabled and static memory is not used, msg is a fixed on-stack array sized RSA_MAX_SIZE/8 + RSA_PSS_PAD_SZ (large enough for the biggest supported modulus plus the 8-byte PSS pad prefix); otherwise msg is a heap pointer initialized NULL and allocated on demand. This dual definition is central to the buffer-size reasoning below.

The salt storage is also configuration-dependent. In the long-salt / salt-length-discover configurations the salt can exceed a single digest, so salt is a pointer that will be aliased into the (heap) msg buffer. In the default configuration the salt is bounded by the digest size, so it lives in a fixed on-stack array of WC_MAX_DIGEST_SIZE bytes. This is a fixed-size buffer paired with the saltLen length field and is therefore a key anchor.

In the long-salt / discover configurations, pkcsBlockLen is used to derive both an allocation size and a discovered salt length, so the code caps it up front at RSA_MAX_SIZE/8 (the largest supported modulus in bytes). This guard bounds every subsequent pkcsBlockLen-derived quantity (the XMALLOC size, the discovered saltLen, maskLen) to the supported maximum, which is exactly what keeps the fixed on-stack msg[] array from overflowing in the no-malloc build. Note this guard only exists in these two configurations.

Resolve the digest size for the chosen hash. wc_HashGetDigestSize returns a negative error for an unsupported hType, which is propagated immediately, so hLen is non-negative past this point. The function then requires the supplied message hash length to exactly equal the digest size ((int)inputLen == hLen); this pins inputLen to a small, known value (at most WC_MAX_DIGEST_SIZE) and removes inputLen as an independent overflow driver in the later copies.

emBits = bits - 1; hiBits is emBits mod 8. When emBits is an exact multiple of 8 (hiBits == 0), the encoded message has a leading zero octet, so the code writes a 0 byte, advances the pkcsBlock cursor past it, and decrements pkcsBlockLen so the rest of the routine operates on the remaining emLen. This mutates both the destination pointer and the working length that drive every later offset (maskLen, the trailer index, the DB XOR position).

Resolve the default salt length. The sentinel RSA_PSS_SALT_LEN_DEFAULT means 'use the digest length as the salt length', so saltLen becomes hLen. A FIPS 186-4 special case: for a 1024-bit modulus with SHA-512, the digest (64) does not leave room for saltLen == hLen, so saltLen is clamped down to RSA_PSS_SALT_MAX_SZ (the maximum that still fits emLen). This branch writes a value into saltLen without going through the saltLen > hLen rejection below, so the fixed salt buffer must be able to hold RSA_PSS_SALT_MAX_SZ.

Validation of an explicitly-supplied saltLen, gated by configuration. In the default build (no long-salt) a caller-supplied saltLen greater than the digest size is rejected, which is what bounds the fixed salt[WC_MAX_DIGEST_SIZE] buffer. Also, negative/out-of-range sentinels below RSA_PSS_SALT_LEN_DEFAULT are rejected. In the discover configuration the sentinel RSA_PSS_SALT_LEN_DISCOVER means 'use the largest salt that fits', computed as pkcsBlockLen - hLen - 2, with an explicit rejection when that goes negative (short block). The (int) cast on pkcsBlockLen is safe here only because line 22 already capped pkcsBlockLen at RSA_MAX_SIZE/8 in this configuration.

The central sizing guard. It enforces pkcsBlockLen >= hLen + saltLen + 2, i.e. the encoded message has room for the DB, the hash H, and the trailer, exactly as EMSA-PSS requires. Because it is done as a signed comparison, a pathologically large pkcsBlockLen whose (int) cast is negative also fails this test, so it doubles as an upper sanity bound. maskLen (the length of the masked-DB region) is then pkcsBlockLen - 1 - hLen; the guard guarantees maskLen >= saltLen + 1 >= 1, which is what keeps the later DB XOR position (line 141) non-negative.

Long-salt / discover assembly of M' = (0x00 x RSA_PSS_PAD_SZ || mHash || salt). A buffer msg of exactly RSA_PSS_PAD_SZ + inputLen + saltLen bytes is allocated (or the fixed on-stack msg[] is used when malloc is disabled). salt, s and m are all aliased to the start of msg. The first RSA_PSS_PAD_SZ bytes are zeroed, then the message hash is copied, then o records the offset of the salt within msg (o = 8 + inputLen). The salt is generated in place at m and m advances by saltLen. Here the salt lives inside msg, so later reads use salt[o + i]. The allocation size is exactly the number of bytes written, and line 22 bounds saltLen so the fixed on-stack variant cannot overflow.

Default-configuration assembly of M'. This branch avoids a separate salt buffer larger than a digest: it either uses a scratch msg (heap, or fixed on-stack in the no-malloc build) when pkcsBlock is too small to hold 8 + inputLen + saltLen, or it reuses pkcsBlock itself as scratch when it is large enough (an in-place optimization). It writes 8 zero bytes, copies the message hash, then generates the salt into the fixed salt[] buffer and XMEMCPYs it into place, with o = 0 so later reads use salt[0 + i]. The reuse-of-pkcsBlock path means the M' scratch and the eventual output share the buffer, which the hashing step relies on being safe.

Compute H = Hash(M') and write it into pkcsBlock at offset maskLen (the position right before the trailer). m - s is the total M' length (8 + inputLen + saltLen). The trailer octet 0xbc (RSA_PSS_PAD_TERM) is written at pkcsBlock[pkcsBlockLen - 1]; the earlier guard guarantees this index is in bounds. RsaMGF then applies MGF1 seeded by the just-written H (hLen bytes) as a mask over the first maskLen bytes of pkcsBlock, producing maskedDB. All three writes are at offsets bounded by the line 72 guard and, when pkcsBlock is reused as scratch, overlap the M' bytes.

Finalize DB. If hiBits is non-zero, the leftmost octet is masked down to hiBits bits so the integer value of EM is < 2^emBits (RFC 8017 step). Then DB is completed: the 0x01 separator is XORed in at pkcsBlock + maskLen - saltLen - 1, and the salt is XORed over the following saltLen bytes. The write offset maskLen - saltLen - 1 is guaranteed non-negative by the line 72 guard, and the highest index touched is maskLen - 1 < pkcsBlockLen. salt[o + i] reads either the fixed salt[] (o == 0) or the salt region inside msg (o == 8 + inputLen), matching where the salt was produced.

Cleanup. In configurations where msg may have been heap-allocated it is freed if non-NULL (msg is NULL when pkcsBlock was reused as scratch, so the guard is correct and there is no double free or leak on the common path). The accumulated ret is returned. Because msg is set NULL at declaration and only assigned by XMALLOC, the free path cannot touch the fixed on-stack msg[] variant (that config compiles the free block out).

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: The entire routine writes at offsets computed from pkcsBlockLen into pkcsBlock: the trailer at pkcsBlock[pkcsBlockLen - 1] (line 131), the hash at pkcsBlock + maskLen (line 127), the MGF mask over maskLen bytes (line 133), and the DB XOR up to pkcsBlock + maskLen - 1 (lines 141-145); and in the reuse branch it also assembles the full 8 + inputLen + saltLen-byte M' into pkcsBlock. Every one of these is bounded only by pkcsBlockLen, never against the actual allocated size of pkcsBlock. The invariant the caller MUST uphold is that pkcsBlock points to a buffer of at least pkcsBlockLen bytes and that pkcsBlockLen equals the key's emLen. If any caller passes a pkcsBlockLen larger than the real allocation (e.g. computed from an attacker-influenced bits/modulus that does not match the buffer), these become out-of-bounds writes of controlled structure into adjacent memory (CWE-787). Internally the guards are self-consistent, so this is a latent contract in the RSA-PSS sign path, not a bug visible in this function alone.

In the default (non-long-salt) configuration the salt is a fixed on-stack array of WC_MAX_DIGEST_SIZE bytes, but it is filled with wc_RNG_GenerateBlock(rng, salt, saltLen) at line 118 and read for saltLen bytes at line 144. saltLen is a length field paired with this fixed buffer. It is clamped to <= hLen by the line 53 check on the explicit-value path (and hLen <= WC_MAX_DIGEST_SIZE), but the FIPS special case at line 48 sets saltLen = RSA_PSS_SALT_MAX_SZ WITHOUT passing through that check. Safety therefore depends on the compile-time invariant RSA_PSS_SALT_MAX_SZ <= WC_MAX_DIGEST_SIZE (in stock wolfSSL RSA_PSS_SALT_MAX_SZ == 62 and WC_MAX_DIGEST_SIZE == 64, so it holds). If a build shrinks WC_MAX_DIGEST_SIZE (e.g. disables SHA-512 sizing) while leaving RSA_PSS_SALT_MAX_SZ, or a future hash raises the salt cap, the RNG write and the salt XOR read overflow/over-read the fixed buffer (CWE-120 / CWE-125). This is the fixed-buffer-vs-length-field contract for salt.

In the WOLFSSL_NO_MALLOC (non-static-memory) build, msg is a fixed on-stack array of RSA_MAX_SIZE/8 + RSA_PSS_PAD_SZ bytes, and the XMALLOC blocks are compiled out so m = msg (lines 85 and 106) points at this stack buffer while the code writes RSA_PSS_PAD_SZ + inputLen + saltLen bytes into it (the zero pad, the hash, and the salt). The invariant is inputLen + saltLen <= RSA_MAX_SIZE/8. In the long-salt/discover path this holds only because line 22 caps pkcsBlockLen (and hence the discovered saltLen) at RSA_MAX_SIZE/8; in the default path it holds because saltLen <= hLen and inputLen == hLen with 2*WC_MAX_DIGEST_SIZE far below RSA_MAX_SIZE/8. If the line 22 guard were ever removed from a long-salt no-malloc build, or RSA_MAX_SIZE were shrunk below the actual key size, this is a stack buffer overflow with attacker-influenced salt bytes (CWE-787 / CWE-121). Confirm the guard is present in every configuration that selects the on-stack msg[].

When hiBits == 0 the code decrements the unsigned word32 pkcsBlockLen without first proving pkcsBlockLen >= 1. If a caller ever passed pkcsBlockLen == 0 with an emBits that is a multiple of 8, this underflows to 0xFFFFFFFF and also writes one byte to pkcsBlock[0] on a zero-length buffer. In practice the wrap is caught downstream: the signed comparison at line 72 evaluates (int)0xFFFFFFFF - hLen == -1 - hLen < saltLen + 2 and returns PSS_SALTLEN_E before any oversized write. So the guard order makes this defended rather than exploitable (CWE-191 latent), but it relies on line 72 being reached; any refactor that consumes pkcsBlockLen as unsigned before that check would expose a huge length. Confirm pkcsBlockLen is always the true key emLen (>= a few dozen bytes) at every call site.

what_could_go_wrong.code:
/* Put Hash at end of pkcsBlock - 1 */
        ret = wc_Hash(hType, s, (word32)(m - s), pkcsBlock + maskLen, hLen);
what_could_go_wrong.explanation: In the reuse branch (m = pkcsBlock, s = pkcsBlock) the M' input region [pkcsBlock, pkcsBlock + 8 + inputLen + saltLen) overlaps the digest output region [pkcsBlock + maskLen, pkcsBlock + maskLen + hLen): when pkcsBlockLen is near the minimum 8 + inputLen + saltLen, maskLen = pkcsBlockLen - 1 - hLen can be smaller than the M' end, so wc_Hash writes its output on top of bytes it also hashes. This is safe only because wc_Hash finalizes over the whole input before writing the digest (no incremental aliasing). The invariant is that wc_Hash treats input and output as potentially overlapping and does not write output until all input is consumed; a streaming/one-shot hash backend that wrote into an aliased output mid-computation would corrupt M' and produce a wrong (or attacker-manipulable) signature. This is a correctness/robustness contract (not a classic overflow); confirm the hash backends used are safe under this aliasing.

The DB completion reads salt[o + i] for i in [0, saltLen). The bound depends on o and the source buffer matching: in the default path o == 0 and salt is the fixed salt[WC_MAX_DIGEST_SIZE] array (safe while saltLen <= WC_MAX_DIGEST_SIZE, see the salt-buffer finding); in the long-salt path o == 8 + inputLen and salt aliases msg, so salt[o + i] indexes msg[8 + inputLen + i], exactly the salt region allocated as RSA_PSS_PAD_SZ + inputLen + saltLen bytes. The invariant is that o and saltLen are consistent with where the salt was actually written (lines 85-95 vs 111-121); the two configurations set o differently, so a mismatch introduced by editing one branch and not the other would cause an out-of-bounds read of the salt (CWE-125). As written the two branches are consistent, so this is a low-risk anchor worth confirming rather than an active bug.

summary: This is the hardened (post-fix) revision of wolfSSL's RsaPad_PSS EMSA-PSS encoder and it is internally memory-safe: a single central guard (pkcsBlockLen >= hLen + saltLen + 2) plus a per-configuration pkcsBlockLen cap and a saltLen <= hLen check keep every computed write/read offset in bounds. The residual risk is entirely contractual/latent: memory safety depends on the caller supplying a pkcsBlock of at least pkcsBlockLen bytes with pkcsBlockLen == the key emLen, on the compile-time relation RSA_PSS_SALT_MAX_SZ <= WC_MAX_DIGEST_SIZE for the fixed salt buffer, on the line-22 cap being present in every no-malloc long-salt build, and on wc_Hash tolerating input/output aliasing in the buffer-reuse path.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: RsaPad_PSS(...) signature (input, inputLen, pkcsBlock, pkcsBlockLen, rng, hType, mgf, saltLen, bits, heap)
reviewed_anchors.reason: pkcsBlock/pkcsBlockLen carry the caller buffer-size contract that all bounds depend on
fixed on-stack scratch buffer filled with RSA_PSS_PAD_SZ+inputLen+saltLen bytes in the no-malloc build
NULL-initialized heap pointer; only ever set by XMALLOC and NULL-checked/freed correctly, no contract violated
aliases into the msg allocation whose size already includes saltLen; bounded by line 22 and the allocation math
fixed-size buffer paired with the saltLen length field; must confirm saltLen never exceeds WC_MAX_DIGEST_SIZE
this cap is the only bound on the on-stack msg[]/discovered saltLen in long-salt builds; its per-config presence is load-bearing
negative error propagated; hLen non-negative and <= WC_MAX_DIGEST_SIZE afterwards
pins inputLen to the digest size, removing it as an independent overflow driver
value in [0,7]; only used to select bit-clearing and the leading-zero path, no unbounded quantity
unsigned decrement without a prior >=1 check; underflow-to-huge is only caught by the later signed guard
hLen <= WC_MAX_DIGEST_SIZE, fits the fixed salt buffer
bypasses the saltLen <= hLen rejection; relies on RSA_PSS_SALT_MAX_SZ <= WC_MAX_DIGEST_SIZE
this is the guard that bounds the fixed salt buffer on the explicit-value path
rejects out-of-range negative sentinels; defensive, no contract broken
discover-mode salt derivation with explicit negative check; (int) cast safe because line 22 caps pkcsBlockLen
rejects invalid sentinels below the discover marker
the central sizing guard; correct and load-bearing, ensures room for DB+H+trailer and non-negative offsets
guarded by line 72 so maskLen >= saltLen+1 >= 1 and < pkcsBlockLen
size exactly matches bytes written; NULL-checked; count is a sum of small bounded values so no multiplication overflow
aliasing setup; all three point into the correctly-sized msg allocation
writes 8+inputLen bytes into a buffer sized 8+inputLen+saltLen, in bounds
records salt offset within msg; consistent with the salt[o+i] read
writes saltLen bytes into the tail of the sized msg allocation

reviewed_anchors.anchor: if (pkcsBlockLen < RSA_PSS_PAD_SZ + inputLen + saltLen) ... else m = pkcsBlock (line 98-110)
reviewed_anchors.reason: chooses in-place reuse of pkcsBlock as scratch, creating the input/output aliasing contract with wc_Hash
same sized allocation and NULL check as the long-salt branch
inputLen == hLen; destination (pkcsBlock reuse or sized msg) proven large enough by line 98/72

reviewed_anchors.anchor: wc_RNG_GenerateBlock(rng, salt, saltLen); XMEMCPY(m, salt, saltLen) (line 118-120)
reviewed_anchors.reason: covered by the salt[WC_MAX_DIGEST_SIZE] finding; destination m has room via the line 98/72 guards

reviewed_anchors.anchor: wc_Hash(hType, s, (word32)(m - s), pkcsBlock + maskLen, hLen) (line 127)
reviewed_anchors.reason: digest output overlaps the M' input in the buffer-reuse path; relies on wc_Hash aliasing safety
index in bounds given pkcsBlockLen>=1 (via line 72); part of the pkcsBlock buffer-size contract already captured

reviewed_anchors.anchor: RsaMGF(mgf, pkcsBlock + maskLen, hLen, pkcsBlock, maskLen, heap) (line 133)
reviewed_anchors.reason: masks maskLen bytes within pkcsBlock; offsets bounded by line 72; delegated MGF1 not shown
single in-bounds byte update; hiBits in [1,7] so the shift is defined
offset non-negative by line 72 (maskLen >= saltLen+1); in-bounds separator write
salt read bound depends on o matching the config; low-risk but a fixed-buffer/index consistency contract
correct guarded free; msg is NULL on the reuse path, no double free or leak

findings.title: pkcsBlock output buffer used as scratch and written at pkcsBlockLen-derived offsets with no size argument for the buffer itself
findings.anchor: m = pkcsBlock; ... pkcsBlock[pkcsBlockLen - 1] = RSA_PSS_PAD_TERM; ... wc_Hash(..., pkcsBlock + maskLen, hLen)
findings.bug_class: Out-of-bounds write via caller buffer-size contract (encoded-message length not validated against actual allocation)
findings.where_it_lives: implementation (file not provided) — the public RSA-PSS sign wrapper that allocates pkcsBlock and computes pkcsBlockLen from the key
findings.invariant: pkcsBlock must point to a writable buffer of at least pkcsBlockLen bytes, and pkcsBlockLen must equal the RSA key's emLen (modulus size in bytes); no argument communicates the buffer's true capacity, so this must be guaranteed at the call site.
findings.failure_mode: If any caller passes a pkcsBlockLen larger than the real pkcsBlock allocation — e.g. derived from a bits/modulus value that does not match the buffer — the trailer write at pkcsBlock[pkcsBlockLen-1], the hash write at pkcsBlock+maskLen, the MGF mask over maskLen bytes, and (in the reuse branch) the full 8+inputLen+saltLen-byte M' assembly write past the buffer, corrupting adjacent memory with attacker-influenced salt/hash structure.
findings.what_to_confirm: At every call site, that pkcsBlock is allocated to >= pkcsBlockLen bytes and pkcsBlockLen is the true key emLen, and that bits is consistent with pkcsBlockLen (hiBits/leading-zero handling assumes bits ~ 8*pkcsBlockLen).

findings.title: FIPS 186-4 SHA-512/1024-bit salt clamp bypasses the saltLen<=hLen check into a fixed WC_MAX_DIGEST_SIZE salt buffer
findings.bug_class: Fixed-size buffer vs length field (stack buffer overflow / over-read if the salt cap exceeds the digest cap)
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/train/train_0069_cwe310.c (this file), gated by build-time size macros
findings.invariant: For the non-long-salt configuration, saltLen must never exceed WC_MAX_DIGEST_SIZE, since the salt lives in byte salt[WC_MAX_DIGEST_SIZE] and is filled by wc_RNG_GenerateBlock(rng, salt, saltLen) (line 118) and read for saltLen bytes at line 144. Concretely RSA_PSS_SALT_MAX_SZ <= WC_MAX_DIGEST_SIZE must hold.
findings.failure_mode: This branch sets saltLen = RSA_PSS_SALT_MAX_SZ without passing through the line-53 saltLen>hLen rejection. In stock wolfSSL RSA_PSS_SALT_MAX_SZ (62) <= WC_MAX_DIGEST_SIZE (64) so it is safe, but a build that shrinks WC_MAX_DIGEST_SIZE (or raises the salt cap) makes the RNG write and the DB XOR overrun the fixed stack salt buffer with attacker-observable salt bytes — a stack overflow / out-of-bounds read.
findings.what_to_confirm: That RSA_PSS_SALT_MAX_SZ is defined <= WC_MAX_DIGEST_SIZE in every build that compiles the fixed salt[WC_MAX_DIGEST_SIZE] variant, and that the SHA-512 special case is only reachable when WC_SHA512_DIGEST_SIZE support keeps WC_MAX_DIGEST_SIZE at 64.

findings.title: Fixed on-stack msg[] scratch buffer in the no-malloc build depends on the RSA_MAX_SIZE cap being present
findings.bug_class: Fixed-size stack buffer vs computed write length (stack buffer overflow if the size cap is absent)
findings.invariant: When msg is the fixed on-stack array, the assembled M' size RSA_PSS_PAD_SZ + inputLen + saltLen must be <= RSA_MAX_SIZE/8 + RSA_PSS_PAD_SZ, i.e. inputLen + saltLen <= RSA_MAX_SIZE/8. In the long-salt/discover path this holds only because line 22 caps pkcsBlockLen (hence the discovered saltLen) at RSA_MAX_SIZE/8.
findings.failure_mode: In a no-malloc long-salt build, msg = XMALLOC is compiled out and m = msg points at the fixed stack array while up to RSA_PSS_PAD_SZ + inputLen + saltLen bytes are written. If the line-22 guard were removed/misconfigured, or RSA_MAX_SIZE were set smaller than the actual key size, the zero-pad/hash/salt writes overflow the stack buffer with partly attacker-influenced content.
findings.what_to_confirm: That the line-22 pkcsBlockLen<=RSA_MAX_SIZE/8 guard is compiled in for every configuration that selects the on-stack msg[] with long-salt, and that RSA_MAX_SIZE matches the largest key the caller can present.

findings.title: Unsigned pkcsBlockLen-- without a prior >=1 check (underflow only caught by a later signed guard)
findings.bug_class: Unsigned integer underflow of a length field
findings.invariant: pkcsBlockLen must be >= 1 before this decrement (in practice it is always the key emLen, tens to hundreds of bytes); the routine relies on the signed comparison at line 72 to reject the wrapped value if this were ever violated.
findings.failure_mode: If pkcsBlockLen were 0 with hiBits==0, the word32 decrement wraps to 0xFFFFFFFF and a byte is written to a zero-length pkcsBlock. The wrap is currently defused because line 72 evaluates (int)0xFFFFFFFF - hLen == -1 - hLen < saltLen+2 and returns before any oversized access, so it is latent; but any change that consumes pkcsBlockLen as unsigned before line 72 (e.g. an allocation sized from it) would turn the wrap into a huge-length primitive.
findings.what_to_confirm: That no call site passes pkcsBlockLen==0, and that line 72's signed comparison remains the first consumer of pkcsBlockLen after this decrement so the wrap stays caught.

findings.title: In-place buffer reuse creates input/output aliasing for wc_Hash
findings.anchor: ret = wc_Hash(hType, s, (word32)(m - s), pkcsBlock + maskLen, hLen);
findings.code: ret = wc_Hash(hType, s, (word32)(m - s), pkcsBlock + maskLen, hLen);
findings.where_it_lives: implementation (file not provided) — the wc_Hash one-shot backends and this reuse path
findings.invariant: wc_Hash must treat its input and output as potentially overlapping and must not write the digest until it has consumed the entire input; in the reuse branch s == pkcsBlock and the output at pkcsBlock+maskLen overlaps the tail of the M' input when pkcsBlockLen is near the minimum.
findings.failure_mode: A hash backend that streamed output into an aliased buffer mid-computation would corrupt the M' bytes it still needs, yielding a wrong digest and thus an invalid or attacker-manipulable PSS signature (a cryptographic-correctness failure rather than a memory-safety one). Standard wolfCrypt one-shot hashes finalize before writing, so this is safe today but is an undocumented aliasing dependency.
findings.what_to_confirm: That every wc_Hash algorithm reachable via hType consumes all input before writing the digest output, so the overlapping in-place reuse of pkcsBlock is safe.

findings.title: salt[o + i] read bound depends on the o/saltLen configuration staying consistent with where the salt was written
findings.anchor: for (i = 0; i < saltLen; i++) { m[i] ^= salt[o + i]; }
findings.bug_class: Out-of-bounds read if the salt offset/source diverge from the assembly path
findings.invariant: o and the salt base must match the branch that produced the salt: default path uses o==0 with the fixed salt[WC_MAX_DIGEST_SIZE] array; long-salt path uses o==8+inputLen with salt aliasing msg. saltLen must not exceed the available bytes at salt+o in whichever configuration is compiled.
findings.failure_mode: As written the two branches are internally consistent (default: salt[0..saltLen) in the fixed buffer bounded by saltLen<=WC_MAX_DIGEST_SIZE; long-salt: msg[8+inputLen .. +saltLen) inside the sized allocation). A maintenance edit that changed o in one branch, or that let saltLen exceed the fixed salt buffer, would read past the salt source and XOR out-of-bounds bytes into DB, leaking heap/stack contents into the (public) encoded message.
findings.what_to_confirm: That o is set consistently in both assembly branches and that saltLen is bounded by the size of the salt source (fixed buffer or msg allocation) in the compiled configuration.

audit_checklist: Confirm every RsaPad_PSS call site allocates pkcsBlock to at least pkcsBlockLen bytes and passes pkcsBlockLen == the key emLen, consistent with bits.
Verify RSA_PSS_SALT_MAX_SZ <= WC_MAX_DIGEST_SIZE in all builds that compile the fixed byte salt[WC_MAX_DIGEST_SIZE] variant (SHA-512/1024 special case).

Verify the line-22 pkcsBlockLen<=RSA_MAX_SIZE/8 cap is compiled in for every WOLFSSL_NO_MALLOC long-salt/discover configuration that uses the on-stack msg[] buffer, and that RSA_MAX_SIZE covers the largest supported key.
Confirm pkcsBlockLen is never 0 at entry so the hiBits==0 decrement at line 40 cannot underflow, and that line 72's signed guard remains the first consumer of the decremented length.
Confirm all wc_Hash backends reachable via hType finalize over the full input before writing output, making the in-place pkcsBlock reuse (line 127) aliasing-safe.
